title: ‘It’s the bill riders pay for 15 years of top sport’ – Fabio Jakobsen on battling Iliac Artery Endofibrosis
content:
Fabio Jakobsen is no stranger to setbacks. During the sprint finish on stage one of the 2020 Tour of Poland, the Dutchman was barged into the race barriers and clattered into the finishing gantry. It was a life-threatening crash, which resulted in the sprinter being placed in a medically-induced coma. Jakobsen returned from his horror crash to win a Tour de France stage in 2022 and is well-versed in cycling’s unglamorous challenges and how to overcome them.

His latest obstacle is Iliac Artery Endofibrosis (IAE), which is a condition where blood flow to the lower legs is restricted due to damage to the arteries that run through the hip and groin area. Jakobsen is one of a number of professional cyclists who have suffered from the issue. Speaking to Rouleur at the AlUla Tour in January, he gave an insight into his struggles with IAE and his plans for yet another comeback. 

Cause and effect

IAE is the product of the repeated exertion on the arteries in the hip and groin area. The extreme hip flexion adopted by cyclists and the high blood flow over time can cause these arteries to kink or the buildup of stiff scar tissue inside the vessels, which both restrict the blood flow through them.

“It's the bill we pay for 15 years of top sport, of building the cardiovascular system because that's what we're doing here – the lungs, the heart, oxygen uptake. Blood flow is the most important thing we have as endurance athletes,” Jakobsen said, explaining why the higher blood flow produced by cyclists can cause unique problems. “I think a big thing is we ride through puberty and we build ‘the engine’. We have got hearts which are twice the size of a normal heart.”

The Picnic PostNL rider said his own turbulent cycling career pathway may have left him more at risk of developing IAE. 

Jakobsen's crash into the barriers on stage one of the 2020 Tour of Poland was life-threatening (Image: Ilario Biondi/SWpix.com)

“I've never considered myself to be extremely talented, so I really have to work for it and do all the training. I think everybody has to, but I definitely had to from 15-16 years old, when you're still growing as a man. I did a couple Grand Tours early in my career. I also had to do a major comeback, after the crash in Poland.”

Jakobsen first started feeling the effects of IAE a couple of seasons ago when he struggled to reach his potential – seemingly inexplicably. 

“I was always dropping first. I couldn't really sprint anymore in the finals. When my quad was in the highest position, the iliac artery kinked, so there was no blood flow. When the leg went down, it opened again. So I had a limited flow, but I didn't have no flow.”

Going backwards

Pinpointing IAE can be tedious because symptoms could point to a number of different possible issues and they only experienced at close to maximum effort and not at rest. Jakobsen’s IAE struggles came at the same time as he moved from Soudal Quick-Step to his current team Picnic PostNL, which made determining the cause even more challenging.

“My training had changed, the team had changed, the material had changed. So, I was looking at those obvious reasons. But then that second year [with Picnic] when I did, in my words, a perfect winter – three months of perfect training and then I came to the AlUla Tour. I got a great lead out from my teammates on stage one. I was in position to finish top three and I started sprinting and it was like I was sprinting backwards. So I finished 11th on the first stage. And then we started looking at what could be going wrong? Because my heart rate was over 190bpm for a minute – I went deep, but I couldn't deliver power and sprint. The feeling was the same as when I was winning races.”

Jakobsen returned from his horror crash in 2020 to win a Tour de France stage in 2022 (Image: Zac Williams/SWpix.com)

This differs from the experience of other IAE sufferers who can get cramp, numbness or swelling. In those January sprints in 2025, Jakobsen didn’t have any acute or obvious indicators of what was wrong. 

“I had exactly the same feeling when going deep whether it's interval training or racing or doing a final up a climb, it's just that the difference is that I didn't recover. I couldn’t get rid of the lactate as fast as the other riders,” Jakobsen explained.

However, Jakobsen is not the first rider to be in this position. IAE, first identified in the 1980s, has gained wider recognition in cycling circles in recent years with a number of riders being diagnosed including Pauline Ferrand-Prévot and Sarah Gigante. IAE can affect either or both legs and vary in its severity due to how damaged the arteries are. Jakobsen and his team were keen to look into it. 

“The diagnosis of the problem is a blood pressure measurement. So they measure at the ankles and after 20-30 minutes of quite a heavy load of riding, they test again,” said Jakobsen.

The CT scan showed that Jakobsen's left (L) and right (R) iliac arteries were severely kinked

This ankle-brachial index (ABI) test is the least invasive option and shows the reduced blood flow to the ankles as a result of the damaged iliac arteries. There are other more expensive options, such as CT or MRA scans. Jakobsen underwent the former to complement his ABI test and they found that he had severe IAE in both legs.

“The blood pressure on the ankles was 38-42% less than higher up the leg,” said Jakobsen. 

Moving forwards

The most effective treatment for the condition is surgery. If the artery is kinked because it’s attached to the psoas muscle then the restrictive fibrous tissue that is binding it should be removed. If the artery walls are narrowed then the surgery involves removing the scar tissue. 

“The doctor said I was still quite early to find it. So it was easily fixable for him, especially because I am young, healthy, with not a lot of fat, because he's normally doing surgery on heavy smokers,” said Jakobsen, whose arteries were kinked and had to be realigned.

“I did a checkup six months later where I did the same blood pressure test as when the diagnosis was made. It was up to 96-98%, so the flow and the pressure is back to normal.”

Given his history, the sprinter is well-placed to follow a rehab program. 

“The key thing is patience. From experience, I know that if you do more, it's not always better. You just need time. You know you need to do the right thing for the right amount of time, which can be three months or six months, but it can also be a year.

“I got the all clear to go and keep racing. To get back to that highest level again just takes time. I'm quite aware of it, but I'm actually extremely happy with how it's going so far.”

Although he hasn’t broken the top ten yet in AlUla or UAE so far this year, Jakobsen is satisfied with how he has felt since his return to racing. Can he get back to winning Grand Tour stages again after this latest setback? Time will tell.
author: Rouleur Media
